topics.title: Binary Trees & BST
topics.story: A family tree — each person (node) has at most two children (left and right). The CEO at the top is the root. Leaves are people with no children. A Binary Search Tree (BST) adds one rule: everyone in your left subtree earns less than you, everyone in your right earns more. This makes finding any person's salary take O(log n) — you eliminate half the company at each level.
topics.analogy: BST is a sorted bookshelf organized as a tree: go left if the book comes alphabetically before the current one, right if after. Finding any book takes O(log n) instead of O(n) linear scan.
topics.memoryAnchor: BST Property: Left < Node < Right. In-order traversal of a BST gives sorted output — use this fact in interviews constantly.
topics.firstPrinciples: A binary tree has at most 2 children per node. Height: O(log n) balanced, O(n) skewed. BST property enables O(log n) search/insert/delete when balanced. Almost every tree problem is solved with DFS (recursive traversal) — identify if it's pre/in/post order.
topics.bruteForce: For search in a tree: BFS/DFS visiting every node O(n). For BST validation: check left < node and right > node locally — WRONG (need to pass min/max bounds). For tree comparison: flatten both to arrays first — more space, same time.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use post-order (bottom-up) for height, diameter, path sum. Use in-order for BST problems (gives sorted order). Pass min/max bounds for BST validation. For LCA: both sides returning non-null means current node is the answer.
topics.whenToUse: Any hierarchical data. BST for sorted dynamic sets. Use BFS for level-order, DFS for path/depth problems. Tree DP (return multiple values from recursion) for complex problems like diameter and path sum.

examples.title: Tree Height using Post-Order
examples.explanation: Post-order: compute left and right heights first (children before parent). The height is 1 + max of both children's heights.

problems.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth (number of nodes along the longest path from root to farthest leaf).

**Example:**
Tree: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: 3
problems.hint1: What is the depth of a null node? What is the depth of a leaf node?
problems.hint2: Recursively compute the depth of the left and right subtrees. The total depth is 1 + max of both.
problems.hint3: Base case: root == null → return 0. Recursive: return 1 + Math.max(depth(root.left), depth(root.right)).
problems.editorial: Classic post-order recursion. O(n) time (visits every node once), O(h) space (recursion stack, h = height). Iterative BFS alternative: count levels using queue size tracking.

problems.title: Invert Binary Tree
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, invert the tree (mirror it) and return its root.

**Example:**
Input:  [4,2,7,1,3,6,9]
Output: [4,7,2,9,6,3,1]
problems.hint1: What does inverting a tree mean at the node level? What needs to swap?
problems.hint2: For each node, swap its left and right children. Then recursively invert both subtrees.
problems.hint3: Pre-order: swap first, then recurse. Or post-order: recurse first, then swap. Both work.
problems.editorial: At each node, swap left and right, then recursively process. O(n) time and space. The recursive call on the right uses the original left (saved in temp) to avoid losing it after the swap.

problems.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid Binary Search Tree (BST).

A valid BST: left subtree < node < right subtree (for ALL descendants, not just immediate children).

**Example:**
Input: [5,1,4,null,null,3,6] → false (4 < 5, but 3 is in the right subtree and 3 < 5)
problems.hint1: Checking only immediate children is not enough. A node in the left subtree must be smaller than ALL its ancestors, not just its parent.
problems.hint2: Pass min and max bounds down the recursion. The root starts with (-∞, +∞). Going left, the upper bound becomes the parent's value. Going right, the lower bound becomes the parent's value.
problems.hint3: validate(node, min, max): return false if node.val <= min or node.val >= max. Recurse left with (min, node.val) and right with (node.val, max).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 10^4 | -2^31 ≤ Node.val ≤ 2^31 - 1 | Use Long for bounds to handle Integer.MIN/MAX values
problems.editorial: The key insight: each node has a valid range inherited from ancestors. Use Long bounds (not int) because node values can be Integer.MIN_VALUE or Integer.MAX_VALUE. Alternative: in-order traversal should be strictly increasing — check that.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of BST
problems.description:
Given a BST and two nodes p and q, find their Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA). The LCA is the lowest node that has both p and q as descendants.

**Example:**
BST = [6,2,8,0,4,7,9,null,null,3,5]
LCA(2,8) = 6
LCA(2,4) = 2
problems.hint1: How does the BST property help? If both p and q are smaller than current node, where must the LCA be?
problems.hint2: If both p and q are less than root → LCA is in left subtree. If both greater → right subtree. Otherwise → root is LCA.
problems.hint3: No need to recurse both sides. The BST property eliminates one side at each step, giving O(log n) for balanced BST.
problems.constraints: 2 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 10^5 | -10^9 ≤ Node.val ≤ 10^9 | All values are unique | p and q are different nodes that exist in the BST
problems.editorial: BST LCA is iterative and O(log n) for balanced trees. The split point where p and q diverge to different sides is the LCA. For general binary tree LCA (not BST), recurse both sides and return the node where both sides are non-null.

problems.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (left to right, level by level).

**Example:**
Input: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[9,20],[15,7]]
problems.hint1: Use a Queue. How do you know when one level ends and the next begins?
problems.hint2: At the start of each iteration, record the queue size — that's the number of nodes in the current level. Process exactly that many nodes.
problems.hint3: Enqueue root. While queue not empty: record size=queue.size(), process 'size' nodes (adding their children), collect their values as one level.
problems.editorial: BFS with level tracking: snapshot queue.size() at start of each while iteration to know the level boundary. O(n) time, O(n) space (queue holds at most one full level at a time, which can be n/2 nodes).

problems.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the length of the diameter (longest path between any two nodes). The path does not need to pass through the root.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,3,4,5]
Output: 3 (path: 4→2→1→3 or 5→2→1→3)
problems.hint1: The diameter through a node = leftHeight + rightHeight. The overall diameter = max diameter across all nodes.
problems.hint2: Use a post-order traversal. At each node, compute the height of left and right subtrees, update the global max diameter, and return height to the parent.
problems.hint3: Maintain a global/instance variable maxDiameter. In the helper: maxDiameter = max(maxDiameter, leftH + rightH). Return 1 + max(leftH, rightH) as the height.
problems.editorial: Tree DP pattern: the helper returns height but also updates a global side-effect (maxDiameter). The diameter at each node = leftHeight + rightHeight. O(n) time, O(h) space. This 'return one thing, update another' pattern appears in many tree problems.

problems.title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
problems.description:
Design an algorithm to serialize (encode) a binary tree to a string and deserialize (decode) it back.

**Example:**
Input tree: [1,2,3,null,null,4,5]
Serialized: '1,2,null,null,3,4,null,null,5,null,null'
Deserialized: same tree structure
problems.hint1: Choose a traversal order that can uniquely reconstruct the tree. Which traversal works for this?
problems.hint2: Pre-order with null markers works — you always know 'root, then left subtree, then right subtree'. Null markers tell you when a subtree is empty.
problems.hint3: Serialize: pre-order DFS, write val or 'null' separated by commas. Deserialize: split by comma, use a pointer/queue, for each value: if null return null, else create node and recurse for left then right.
problems.editorial: Pre-order with null markers uniquely reconstructs the tree. The idx variable advances through the flattened array in sync with the recursive reconstruction. O(n) time and space for both operations.

problems.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
problems.description:
A path in a binary tree is a sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes has an edge. The path does not need to pass through the root. Find the maximum sum of any path.

**Example:**
Input: [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: 42 (path: 15→20→7)
problems.hint1: The max path through a node uses: left contribution + node.val + right contribution. But can a contribution be negative?
problems.hint2: Each subtree's contribution to the parent's path is max(0, subtree_max_path). If the subtree contributes negatively, skip it (treat as 0).
problems.hint3: Helper returns the max gain from this subtree (path ending at this node extending upward). Internally updates global maxSum with left+node+right.
problems.editorial: Tree DP: gain(node) returns the maximum path sum starting at node going downward (can only go one direction — either left or right, not both, since it's a path to a parent). Internally, we consider the full path through the node (left + node + right) and update global max. O(n) time and space.

problems.title: Path Sum II
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree and a target sum, return all root-to-leaf paths where the sum of node values equals targetSum.

**Example:**
Tree: [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2,null,null,5,1], targetSum=22
Output: [[5,4,11,2],[5,8,4,5]]
problems.hint1: This is a path enumeration problem. You need to track the current path and add it to results when you reach a valid leaf.
problems.hint2: DFS backtracking: add node to path, subtract from remaining sum. At a leaf, if remaining==0, add path copy to results. Then recurse left and right. Remove node from path on the way back.
problems.hint3: A leaf is a node with no children (node.left == null && node.right == null). Don't add internal nodes that hit the sum — only leaves count.
problems.editorial: DFS backtracking: add node → recurse → remove node. Only record paths at leaves where remaining sum == node.val. O(n) time, O(n) space for path + output. Deep copy the path before adding to results — otherwise all entries point to the same mutated list.

problems.title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
problems.description:
Given the root of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal of its nodes' values. Level 1 goes left→right, level 2 right→left, alternating.

**Example:**
Input: [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]
Output: [[3],[20,9],[15,7]]
problems.hint1: This is level-order traversal with alternating direction. How do you efficiently reverse alternate levels?
problems.hint2: BFS with level tracking. For even levels, add nodes left-to-right. For odd levels, add right-to-left (or reverse the collected level).
problems.hint3: Use a flag or check level%2. Use a Deque for the level's values — for odd levels, addFirst() instead of add().
problems.editorial: BFS with LinkedList as level collector — addLast() for L→R, addFirst() for R→L. Toggle direction after each level. O(n) time, O(n) space (queue holds at most n/2 nodes at one time).

problems.title: Count Complete Tree Nodes
problems.description:
Given the root of a complete binary tree, return the number of nodes. A complete binary tree is filled at every level except the last, which is filled from left to right.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,3,4,5,6] → Output: 6

Do better than O(n).
problems.hint1: Brute force is O(n). A complete binary tree has a special property — can you use it to avoid visiting every node?
problems.hint2: Compare left height and right height. If they're equal, the left subtree is a perfect binary tree with 2^h - 1 nodes (computable in O(1)). Recurse only on the right.
problems.hint3: getLeftHeight: go left until null. getRightHeight: go right until null. If leftH == rightH: left subtree is perfect, add 2^leftH - 1 + 1 (root), recurse right. Else: right subtree is perfect with height leftH-1, recurse left.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 5*10^4 | -10^4 ≤ Node.val ≤ 10^4 | The tree is guaranteed to be complete
problems.editorial: O(log²n): at each level, O(log n) to compute heights, O(log n) recursion depth. When leftH == rightH, left subtree is a perfect binary tree — count without traversal using 2^h-1. Either left or right subtree is always perfect in a complete tree.

problems.title: Binary Tree Cameras
problems.description:
Place cameras on some nodes of a binary tree. Each camera monitors its parent, itself, and its children. Find the minimum number of cameras needed to monitor all nodes.

**Example:**
Input: [0,0,null,0,0] → Output: 1
Input: [0,0,null,0,null,0,null,null,0] → Output: 2
problems.hint1: Greedy insight: it's always better to place cameras as high as possible (at parents rather than leaves). Try placing cameras bottom-up.
problems.hint2: Each node returns one of 3 states: 0=not covered, 1=has camera, 2=covered (no camera). A camera is placed when a child returns 0 (not covered).
problems.hint3: Post-order DFS. If any child returns 0 → place camera here (return 1, increment count). If any child has camera (returns 1) → this node is covered (return 2). Else → not covered yet (return 0). After DFS, if root returns 0 → place camera at root.
problems.editorial: Greedy post-order: leaves never get cameras (parent is better placement). Return states: 0=uncovered (force parent to add camera), 1=has camera, 2=covered. If root is still uncovered after DFS, add one more camera. O(n) time, O(h) space.
